$comment: Telemetry collectors that work with RacePlex, and how.
This file is plain data. It is the source for the in-app 'Supported Devices' dialog and the
table in the README. Anyone can edit it; to add a device, open a pull request.
What RacePlex reads: GPX, VBO, NMEA, UBX, GoPro .mp4 (GPS from the video's GPMF track), and CSV.
CSV covers the named formats (RaceBox, VESC Tool, MoTeC, AiM, Alfano, Dove) and, through the
generic importer, most other delimited logs with a latitude and a longitude. An unknown CSV has
its delimiter detected and its columns mapped by name, and you can correct the mapping before
A 'no' status means the app produces no exportable file, or only a proprietary binary we cannot
RacePlex sells no hardware and has no affiliation with any of these vendors. Prices are
approximate, in USD, and will go out of date. Sample rate is the number that matters most.
status: 'verified'  — we have parsed a real file from this device
'expected'  — it exports a format we read; nobody has sent us a file yet
'partial'   — works through a conversion step or a companion app; see notes

categories.name: Dedicated GPS performance meters
categories.blurb: Purpose-built GPS loggers. Sample rate matters more for eskate than for cars, because a run often lasts under a minute: at 40 km/h a 1 Hz fix lands every 11 metres, while 25 Hz puts one every 44 cm.

devices.name: RaceBox Micro
devices.notes: The best option for eskate. Records standalone with a hardware button, so no phone is involved while you ride, and it is small enough to tape to a deck. Exports GPX, VBO and CSV. Our test fixtures come from one.

devices.name: RaceBox Mini / Mini S
devices.notes: Same data as the Micro; you are paying for form factor, battery and mounts. Needs the phone app to record. Export goes through RaceBox's cloud, so you need an account and internet to get the file out.

devices.name: Dragy DRG70 / DRG70-C / Pro
devices.notes: Dragy has no CSV export; the vendor confirms this. Use it as a Bluetooth GPS source for RaceChrono and export from there, or use the dragy·Lap app's .vbo export. The original 2018 Dragy ran at 10 Hz; the current DRG70 reaches 25 Hz.

devices.name: Qstarz LT-6000S
devices.notes: Export GPX from the QRacing desktop software (Windows only). Its CSV should now also import through the generic column mapper.

devices.name: VBOX Sport
devices.notes: Exports .vbo natively. It costs about three times a RaceBox for a lower sample rate, and availability is poor, so it is mainly worth using if you already own one.

devices.name: Bare u-blox module (DIY)
devices.notes: The cheapest RacePlex-compatible logger there is. Any u-blox M8/M10 dumping raw NMEA or UBX to an SD card drops straight in — both parsers already exist, no conversion needed.

categories.name: Phone apps
categories.blurb: A phone works if you already have one, but its built-in GPS runs at about 1 Hz, which is coarse for a short run. Pairing one of these apps with an external Bluetooth GPS receiver raises the rate to 10–25 Hz.

devices.name: RaceChrono Pro
devices.rateHz: 1 Hz on phone GPS · 25 Hz with an external receiver
devices.notes: The best software path, and it drives a RaceBox or Dragy over Bluetooth at full rate. Export VBO, NMEA or GPX — NOT its CSV, which is RaceChrono's own layout and will not parse.

devices.name: Harry's LapTimer
devices.rateHz: 1 Hz on phone GPS · higher with an external receiver
devices.notes: GPX from any edition; VBO from the Petrolhead edition up.

devices.name: Strava / generic GPX loggers
devices.notes: Works, and a good way to check RacePlex with a file you already have. At 40 km/h a 1 Hz fix is one point every 11 metres, which is enough for a route trace but not for lap deltas.

devices.name: TrackAddict
devices.notes: Should import through the generic column mapper (its '#' comment preamble and quoted header are handled). Note it samples at 50 Hz but only updates GPS at ~10 Hz, so honour its GPS_Update flag if the trace looks stepped. Send us a file and we will mark it verified.

categories.name: External Bluetooth GPS receivers
categories.blurb: Feed a phone app a proper 10–25 Hz fix instead of the phone's own 1 Hz. This is the single biggest upgrade available to anyone logging with a phone.

devices.name: RaceBox Mini / Micro (as a receiver)
devices.notes: Does both jobs: logs standalone, or streams to RaceChrono at 25 Hz. Best of both.

devices.name: Garmin GLO 2
devices.notes: Export GPX from the vendor's website. FIT is the native format for these devices and carries more data; RacePlex cannot read it yet (see the open issues). All of them log at about 1 Hz.

devices.name: Dual XGPS160 SkyPro
devices.notes: Use with RaceChrono or Harry's LapTimer.

categories.name: Action cameras with GPS
categories.blurb: GoPro records GPS into the video file. Drop the .mp4 in and RacePlex reads the telemetry track in the browser; there is no conversion step and nothing is uploaded.

devices.name: GoPro HERO5–11, HERO13, MAX
devices.notes: Import the .mp4 straight into RacePlex — the GPS rides along in the video's GPMF metadata track and we read it in the browser (nothing is uploaded, no conversion step). Accelerometer and gyro come along too. GPS must have been switched on in Preferences > Regional > GPS before recording, and the camera needs a lock before you hit record.

devices.name: GoPro HERO12
devices.notes: Has no GPS receiver. GoPro removed it for this model.

devices.name: Insta360 (with GPS remote)
devices.notes: Only records GPS with a GPS Action Remote / Smart Remote (or the phone app). Insta360 Studio then has an 'Export GPX File' option.

categories.name: Watches & bike computers
categories.blurb: Useful for trying RacePlex with a file you already have. They log at about 1 Hz, which is too coarse for lap timing.

devices.name: Garmin / Wahoo / Coros
devices.notes: Export GPX from the vendor's website. FIT is the native format for these devices and carries more data; RacePlex cannot read it yet (see the open issues). All of them log at about 1 Hz.

categories.name: Eskate & VESC
categories.blurb: VESC Tool logs import directly, and bring the ESC channels with them: motor current, battery sag, duty cycle and ERPM are charted on the same timeline as GPS. The other eskate apps import through the generic CSV path.

devices.name: VESC Tool
devices.notes: Imports directly, with the ESC channels: motor current, battery sag, duty cycle, ERPM and temperatures are charted alongside GPS. A nosedive shows up as a duty-cycle spike. The ESC logs at about 12 Hz and the GNSS at about 1 Hz; RacePlex keeps every ESC row at full rate and interpolates position between GPS fixes. Verified against a real Onewheel ride.

devices.name: FreeSK8 / Robogotchi
devices.notes: Its shared CSV is row-typed (each line prefixed with its record type), so the generic importer may not take it as-is — send us one and we will finish it. Caveat: ~1 Hz GPS, and the project has been quiet since 2022.

devices.name: Metr Pro
devices.notes: Its CSV export should import through the generic column mapper. The JSON export (?format=json on a ride URL) is column-oriented arrays and needs its own small parser — send us one. Note Metr also supports ?format=fit.

devices.name: Float Control / pOnewheel
devices.notes: Both export CSV and import through the generic column mapper. pOnewheel generates its columns per ride from whichever BLE attributes it recorded, so the mapper is the only route for it. Float Control also changes its column order between versions, which name-based mapping handles. Send us a file and we will mark these verified.

devices.name: Official Onewheel app
devices.notes: Records GPS but has no export function, so there is no file to import. Use a separate GPS logger, or Float Control.
